title: EIP-8125: Temporary Contract Storage
description: Introduces temporary storage for contracts via TMPLOAD and TMPSTORE opcodes. Data persists across transactions and blocks but is automatically cleared on a protocol-defined schedule using a two-period rollover between two reserved system accounts. Provides bounded-lifetime semantics without explicit deletes, targeting use cases where data is valuable only for a limited time window.
author: Wei Han Ng (@weiihann)
laymanDescription: Adds a new type of contract storage that automatically expires after a set number of blocks. Over 60% of storage slots are written once and never read again, wasting space permanently. This lets contracts store short-lived data cheaply, knowing the protocol will clean it up automatically.

endUsers.description: Indirect benefit: contracts using temporary storage may offer lower gas costs for operations involving short-lived data.

appDevs.description: New TMPLOAD/TMPSTORE opcodes enable storing ephemeral data cheaply; developers must handle automatic expiry and cannot assume indefinite retention.

walletDevs.description: No direct wallet changes required; wallets may surface temporary storage reads in transaction previews if contracts adopt the new opcodes.

toolingInfra.description: Block explorers, debuggers, and indexers must support the new opcodes and account for data expiry when displaying temporary storage state.

layer2s.description: No direct integration needed. L2s deriving from the EVM may choose to adopt temporary storage for their own state management benefits.

stakersNodes.description: Node operators benefit from bounded state growth; temporary storage is automatically pruned, reducing long-term disk and I/O requirements.

elClients.description: Must implement two new opcodes, two reserved system accounts, periodic storage root clearing at period boundaries, and new gas metering logic.

clClients.description: No consensus-layer logic changes; only standard hard fork activation coordination with execution clients is required.

benefits: Reduces long-term state growth by automatically clearing storage that contracts no longer need.
Provides cheaper gas costs for ephemeral data compared to permanent SSTORE/SLOAD.
Guarantees a minimum retention period, giving developers a predictable expiry model.
Requires no explicit cleanup by contracts, eliminating a class of state-management bugs.
tradeoffs: Adds two new opcodes and system accounts, increasing EVM complexity for client implementers.
Contracts must handle the case where temporary data has expired unexpectedly.
Many gas cost parameters remain TBD, making economic impact difficult to assess currently.

scaleL1.description: Directly reduces long-term state bloat by giving contracts a built-in mechanism for bounded-lifetime storage, lowering node resource requirements.
